item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I'm necroposting, but…

"evil is inherent in this thing's biology."

I'm sorry, but this is just another tired cliche that I hate. Yes, there are a great deal of corrupt corporations out there, but the good:bad ratio is blown way out of proportion in the media than it is in real life.

The idea that just being a business automatically make you evil is just as illogical as the idea that being a hivemind automatically makes you evil. 1442 has already deconstructed the latter, it can quite easily deconstruct the former if allowed to take root in the lore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I'm necroposting, but it having a unique relationship with it's own CEO would contradict the ideas and premises established in it's own article. How often do you see a person who has a "unique relationship" with a particular one of their own brain cells?

…wait hold on that could be a great SCP in it's own right

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. a great deconstruction of the concept of a hivemind. most hiveminds in media are always hostile and driven solely by assimilation; I always hated how rare benign and benevolent ones are, since there's no real reason for them to tend towards malevolence so often.

What's more, I normally hate slashfiction, but trust me when I say that it's really saying something that I want to see LOTS of slashfiction of this. I want to see it have a merger and live happily ever after with Pepsico, then give birth to a hundred little subsidaries with them. I want to see a flashback to it's highschool years where Microsoft valiantly saved it from it's abusive relationship with Enron. I want to see it join the ever-growing cast of Disney's Harem comedy.

THAT'S how big an upvote I'm giving you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always nice when something like this turns out not to be hostile. Pleasant surprise and easy plus 1
Someday I shall write a tale about this little fella.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Corporations Are People, Too!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has fantastic buildup and the payoff is great. You’re expecting something sinister but are rewarded with something sweet instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the addendum, the impact it had on me was an instant +1.
There might be some kind of anomaly in this page… or addendum that inclines us to rate this page, haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The short answer is because I figured the Foundation has automated systems checking for unusual patterns in all streams of publicly available data.

The longer answer involves some of my personal headcanon about how the Foundation operates. My theory is the Foundation has some understanding about how anomalies tend to form and why, and that the theoretical possibility of something like this existing had been established in Foundation theoretical literature long before its discovery, and this theory was at least part of the motivation for analyzing stock trades for this kind of pattern in the first place.

The non-diegetic answer is "because having come up with the idea of a sentient corporation, I needed a way for the Foundation to find it and this was the best I could think of".

item.title: Re: +200!
item.content:encoded: That was a message they intercepted, not necissarily one directed at them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why were they looking for codes in the stock market to begin with?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FUCK ITS SO CUTE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't want to necro waterfire's post but it would be super cool to see some interactions between one or several of the companies that the SCP foundation owns and 1442 either in a tale or extended test log. I just hope they don't lead it on too much.

…the Foundation currently holds a minority █.█% share.

you bought 2.6% of me why

Hmmmm…

EDIT: Just now noticed I have the honor of being +1 number 200! congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Employees experience their behaviour as freely-willed, but are unusual in reporting higher levels of job satisfaction than the statistical baseline for their economic sector; staff interviews typically feature self-reports of high levels of motivation, strong interpersonal relationships while at work, and a sense of "being part of something larger".

And this is where I laughed my ass off and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wishun's much earlier comment made me wonder what the Foundation might find if it began looking for similar communication-priming patterns EVERYWHERE. Sure you'd get false positives due to stochasticy, but those could be checked. You might find these gestaltic entities in stock markets, non-profits, nations, industrial agriculture, etc.
…Actually, this proposal would definitely be rejected.
"So what you hypothesize, Doctor, is that the world may be full of untold multitudes of sapient entities that we could detect, but wouldn't be normally noticed, and couldn't be contained. Why should we create this much non-productive work for ourselves?"

Enthusiastic +1,BTW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I see! Very neat! I like it, a lot.

How many other corporations are conscious, and we just haven't decoded their communication, I wonder?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has a governance structure; a managing director, a CEO, a board of directors, shareholders, the whole shebang. It's in every respect a normal corporation in that sense.

The idea is that nevertheless the whole entity, inclusive of said senior management, but also inclusive of all the rank and file workers, the copy machines, the coffee machines, their accounts for the year 2007 on file paper, Gareth's office birthday party at the Omaha office, the fact that someone just coughed on the 47th floor of HQ, the particular arrangement of some printer cables, the exact number of oxygen atoms in the men's restroom and everything else that makes up the corporation together, collectively and irreducibly constitutes its own consciousness. In an analogous way to how each of you neurons and synapses fire according to base physical laws, but nevertheless taken collectively constitute a sentience.

Where the management ends and the entity begins is as such an unanswerable question. It isn't straightforwardly separable like that, any more than we could remove a chunk of your brain and locate definitively within it your sense of self.

The one thing that is for sure is that the CEO or the directors or whatever don't directly order the stock trades which code into messages. I imagine instead they arise through subtle actions: tired workers, programming errors, slips of the finger, internal miscommunication, coffee spilt on keyboards, individual whims; minor events that if traced back would actual be themselves the product of a hundred other minor events which themselves were each the product of another hundred etcetera etcetera. The conciousness is only detectable by considering the whole, and the patterns of the whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this a lot, but as a man who works for a similarly-sized organisation, I know that employees are constantly bombarded by emails/bulletins/news summaries/board reports from the CEO/board/etc. Does this corp have a governance structure? Where does it end and the entity begin? If it's the case that, say, there is no-one over senior management, is it part of the anomalous effect that none of the employees notice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Carl Sagan's book Contact (and the associated movie) features as a plot point a signal from extraterrestrials which is first detected as being such because it features a series of prime numbers. This method is frequently posited as a way to communicate that signal is coming from an intelligent source rather than a natural one, after earlier confusion over eg. quasars, which were at one stage thought to be a possible extraterrestrial signal, which is why I used it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what are you referring to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Contact, by Carl Sagan. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn…I've had a really similar idea in my sandbox for a while now — digits of pi forming coded messages, implying a consciousness.

I laughed at "a sense of being part of something larger". Loved the addendum, too. +1

item.title: Re: 100+
item.content:encoded:
Jesus, this hit +100 in a liitle over a month…

Congrats!

item.title: Re: 100+
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all. I couldn't be more pleased with how this has been received.

Of course, I've now had over a month's worth of being completely blocked in coming up with another skip to follow it. Ah well. If it comes to pass that this is my only contribution to the skipverse, I'm glad it was liked :).

EDIT: I'm very keen to see tales if people want to do them. Consider this post permission to anyone do one if you want to! I'll even help out if that'd be useful; I'm often in #site19 if you wanna ask questions, bounce ideas, or run drafts by me.
Congratulations! Now will someone finally make a tale about this?

item.title: Really nice.
item.content:encoded: Very creative. -1 +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me squeal "Oh my god" at the end. Great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I WAS JUST ABOUT TO SAY THAT, DAMMIT

Seriously, though, this SCP is pretty awesome. I'm sure a lot of people have had similar ideas, but you actually made it work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Corporations really are people too, apparently. I wonder if Mitt realises what he created.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you turn Citizens United into a skip?

Did it freaking a/s/l another corporation?

It's lonely. ;_; All my feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The +1 I gave you already is now even more deserved than before. I missed that entirely. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The message transcript wasn't intended to necessarily be directed at the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One quibble, you blackboxed the percentage of the company that the Foundation owns but then revealed it in the addendum… unless they bought more since then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really well executed - I'd second the opinions of other commentators that I'm glad you didn't go down the Skynet route and make it unnecessarily grimdark. I didn't spot the comment about whether it recognises humans as sentient or not when I read it on your sandbox, but it's a really nice touch; presumably for an emergent intelligence based on a human substrate, it would be as natural to recognise humans as sentient as it would be for humans to recognise their own brain cells as such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats, you found my easter egg :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Est 1984? Based in Boston? Weird and very happy-smiley staff? First word four letters, second word seven letters?

Do I spot some Bain Capital/Mitt Romney/Mormons references in here somewhere?

item.title: Re: Well slap me with a tuna!
item.content:encoded:
All I heard was "drunken frat party".

You get SCP-1626, I'll get SCP-420-J.

item.title: Re: Well slap me with a tuna!
item.content:encoded: So on the plane of writing, when given points X, Y, and Z, and a conclusion on that axis, should we instead have a drunken frat party of a conclusion at point E?

item.title: Well slap me with a tuna!
item.content:encoded:
That's not at all surprising at this point. It's becoming drearily expected.

So the anti-cliche has become a cliche itself. Actually this makes complete sense — the reverse of an obvious thing is still fairly obvious…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: analyzing instead of analysis?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought that was adorable, and it's the main thing that spurred me to upvote, because the article itself was pretty confusing to me due to being about such arcane things as stock exchanges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a lovely, lovely idea. I was fully expecting it to go either grimdark or slapstick; I was pleasantly surprised.

And the Foundation had better keep an eye on this thing, because eventually it's liable to realize its potential power. And sometimes "lonely" segues into "crazy" before anyone notices…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a probably terrible idea for this that you probably shouldn't listen to…

Communicate with it by encoding messages into the stock exchanges of a Foundationfront company!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Childlike", you say? hmmmm….
Well, it was born in 1984, thirty years is very young by the standards of large corporations. Add in that it is presumed to "live" at a much slower pace than humans and 1442 might be an effective 15 year old, or even younger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. I'm hesitant to mess drastically with it at this point. Maybe a tale…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is also a factor that 1442 was not trying to hide its messages but rather doing its level best to make them as easy to understand as possible.

Even the dumbest pattern recognition software and/or analyst is going to spot a string of primes, provided anyone is thinking to look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I can see a good way to integrate this critique — it'd make the article even better, IMO. Just remove the chatspeak and the immaturity. Keep the loneliness, but make the expression thereof a little less silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For what it's worth, I think that "mind" (with quotes) is both a fitting word choice and scientifically sound terminology.

"Challenging" and "subtle" are also go-to adjectives in real life scientific journals and papers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. I laughed hard at the end, and then I thought "…waitaminute."

Anyways, fun stuff, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll agree that the subversion is obvious at this point, and even a bit tiring, though I don't think I'm as bothered by it as you are. But I'd argue that it works in this case. The rudimentary, childlike personality the corporation affects at the end doesn't defuse the inherent menace in the concept, and if anything actually underlines it: This emergent entity is vulnerable, gentle, and even sweet, but, despite that, is still a shadowy, dangerous multinational with its fingers in the arms industry, heavy mineral extraction, and pharmaceuticals (not to mention the ominous-by-proximity children's toy retail). The "subversion" at the end changes nothing, and by its intensity even draws attention to how incongruous and self-defeating it is. Evil is a part of this thing's biology. So what if it's cutesy? This is strong enough to work on the level of commentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. I thought it was kinda cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This.

Also, did anyone else notice that one of the addendum messages was the sentient corporation equivalent to "A/S/L"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know an SCP is good when Bright upvotes it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chat Roulette stock trader? Here, have this +1 for being clever and well written.

I'm particularly impressed by the containment procedure here: it basically contains itself as long as no former cryptanalysts take a close look at their trading history. A lot of newbies would have overshot and had the Foundation just flat-out purchase the company and replace everyone with agents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet, the author catches you by surprise and slaps you with a tuna filled with feels and humor.

That's not at all surprising at this point. It's becoming drearily expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I completely understand!

I will say: I did try to go in a grimdark, ominous direction with this initially - you can see it in the first draft on my sandbox - but I just couldn't get it to work. Loneliness proved to be the emotional hook I was able to hit with this, in a way that didn't come off as too forced. And I do think an entity like this would be lonely, although the precise manner of its articulation of that is, absolutely, self-consciously absurd.

The article is what is is now, and I absolutely wouldn't begrudge anyone not finding it their cup of tea. It remains my foremost desire to create a truly creepy, even terrifying, SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I liked this because it had that ending. After reading the description, you'd expect the Corporation to be some dark, powerful sentient sort of creature (though that's a bit cliche). Yet, the author catches you by surprise and slaps you with a tuna filled with feels and humor.
You have your own standards, though, so if you don't like it you don't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All right, time to grump up this thread.

I don't advise changing this in any way, since you've gotten an overwhelmingly positive reception. And indeed, it's a well-written article with a unique premise. However, this proved to be a really disappointing read for me.

I mean, the concept of a sentient organization of people is really cool. And it doesn't go the necessarily obvious route with some sort of classical hive-mind approach. And it doesn't communicate in a directly obvious way, either. Great approach to giving us something truly different, which is very hard to do here, even for experienced writers.

So, the payoff comes next. What does this bizarre, unreal entity do? What does it want? This could go in any number of directions, and we end up with "lol do u like me". This reality-breaking, anomalous entity that is utterly inhuman acts like a lonely teenager on the internet. Off all the things it could do, this was possibly the most insipid and disappointing option. It was like watching a gymnast execute maneuver after maneuver, only to completely faceplant on the dismount.

It's not your fault, really. You've obviously done your homework and approached the process the way you should. But you've done your job too well, in some respects. There are trends in how articles are written, and the current one right now is to subvert the traditional horror of the wiki (dismissively referred to as "grimdark" these days), usually by provoking other emotional responses such as sympathy or sadness. That's fine, and there's places for that, but in your studious attempt to pick up on modern standards (which is admirable, by the way), I feel you've absorbed this need to avert the cliche. In doing so, though, the reactionary response becomes a cliche itself, and what could have been a natural, satisfying ending to this (and not necessarily one filled with horror or doom, either) was replaced with complete silliness.

I'm a picky fucker, and you've got plenty of upvotes, so take what I say with a grain of salt. But this was too inconsistent and too jarring at the end for me to upvote in good conscience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, even better by the standards of most first SCPs! +1
